в‘Ј Infraestructura e InyecciГіn de Dependencias
When to use an array and when to use an arraylist?
Estructura proyectos AI con Clean Architecture вЂ” separaciГіn de capas, inyecciГіn de dependencias y testing sin infraestructura.

documents.t: Domina Python a nivel avanzado para proyectos de AI вЂ” tipado estГЎtico, async, NumPy vectorizado y profiling.
El ecosistema de herramientas AI evoluciona rГЎpidamente. Esta es la selecciГіn curada del stack mГЎs relevante.
El ecosistema de herramientas AI evoluciona rГЎpidamente. Esta es la selecciГіn curada del stack mГЎs relevante.
Este mГіdulo organiza estructuras de datos en dos bloques principales:
An `array` stores contiguous elements in memory, allowing access by index in constant time.
An `ArrayList` (also known as a Dynamic Array) is a data structure that uses an internal Array to store elements, but has the ability to automatically resize itself.
A Deque, short for Double-Ended Queue, is a linear data structure that allows you to insert and delete elements from both the front and the rear.

If the Array was a fixed row of boxes and the ArrayList was an elastic row, the LinkedList is something completely different: imagine a treasure hunt. Each clue gives you an object and a piece of paper that tells you where to find the next clue.
A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First-In, First-Out) principle, which means that the first item to enter is the first to leave.
A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) principle, which means that the last element to enter is the first to leave.
Un BST es un binary tree con regla de orden:
Un binary tree es una estructura jerГЎrquica donde cada nodo tiene como mГЎximo dos hijos: izquierdo y derecho.
AI Architect GuГ­a tГ©cnica para diseГ±ar sistemas AI escalables, mantenibles y seguros. El AI Architect toma decisiones estratГ©gicas que impactan cГіmo los modelos se integran en productos reales.

A diferencia del AI Engineer (que implementa), el Architect diseГ±a el sistema: define cГіmo los componentes se conectan, quГ© tecnologГ­as usar y cГіmo garantizar escalabilidad, seguridad y gobernanza.
documents.s: ВїQuГ© hace un AI Software Architect?

documents.t: Fase DuraciГіn Temas Prerrequisito Variable Clean Arch, Microservicios, Cloud, Seguridad Fase 2 2вЂ“4 meses ComprensiГіn de modelos, Data Arch, MLOps Platform Fase 3 3вЂ“5 meses RAG Arch, Multi-Agent, LLM Gateway, Vector DBs Fase 4 2вЂ“3 meses GPU Infra, Inference Optimization, Scalability Fase 5 Continuo AI Governance, AI Security, FinOps, Leadership
Empieza por Clean Architecture вЂ” es el fundamento que unifica todo lo demГЎs.

AI Architect В· FundamentoAmbos roles Clean Architecture, popularizada por Robert C. Martin ("Uncle Bob"), proporciona una estructura de proyecto que separa responsabilidades, facilita el testing y permite cambiar tecnologГ­as (modelos, bases de datos, APIs) sin reescribir la lГіgica de negocio. Tags: Clean Architecture В· DDD В· Dependency Inversion В· Hexagonal В· Use Cases
documents.s: рџ§№ Clean Architecture para Proyectos AI

documents.t: La regla fundamental es que las dependencias de cГіdigo solo pueden apuntar hacia adentro: las capas externas dependen de las internas, nunca al revГ©s. La pregunta que Clean Architecture responde ВїPuedo cambiar de GPT-4 a LLaMA sin tocar mi lГіgica de negocio? ВїPuedo cambiar de Pinecone a Qdrant sin reescribir mis casos de uso? ВїPuedo testear mi lГіgica de RAG sin conectarme a ninguna API? Si la respuesta es "no" a cualquiera, tienes un problema de arquitectura.
documents.s: El Concepto Central: La Regla de Dependencia

documents.t: Capa DescripciГіn En proyectos AI в‘  Dominio Entidades y reglas de negocio puras. Sin dependencias. Document, Chunk, Embedding, SearchResult в‘Ў Casos de Uso LГіgica de aplicaciГіn. Orquesta entidades. IndexDocuments, QueryRAG, RunAgent в‘ў Adaptadores Convierte entre mundo externo y casos de uso. REST controllers, CLI, gRPC handlers в‘Ј Infraestructura Detalles tГ©cnicos: DBs, APIs, frameworks. OpenAI client, Pinecone, SQLAlchemy, FastAPI

ai-service/ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ src/ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ domain/ # в‘  NГљCLEO вЂ” sin dependencias externas в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ entities/ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ document.py # class Document в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ chunk.py # class Chunk в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ message.py # class ConversationMessage в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ search_result.py # class SearchResult в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ repositories/ # Interfaces (Protocols) в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ document_repo.py # Protocol: save, get, delete в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ vector_store.py # Protocol: index, search, delete в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ llm_gateway.py # Protocol: complete, embed в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ application/ # в‘Ў CASOS DE USO в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ use_cases/ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ index_documents.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ query_rag.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ run_agent.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ summarize.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ services/ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ chunking_service.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ reranking_service.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ adapters/ # в‘ў ADAPTADORES в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ api/ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ routers/ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ documents.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ chat.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ schemas/ в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ request.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ response.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ cli/ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ commands.py в”‚ в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ infrastructure/ # в‘Ј INFRAESTRUCTURA в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ llm/ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ openai_gateway.py в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ anthropic_gateway.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ ollama_gateway.py в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ vector_stores/ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ pinecone_store.py в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ qdrant_store.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ chroma_store.py # Para testing/dev в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ repositories/ в”‚ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ postgres_doc_repo.py в”‚ в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ inmemory_doc_repo.py # Para testing в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ config/ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ settings.py в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ container.py # Dependency injection в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ tests/ в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ unit/ # Sin IO вЂ” rГЎpidos в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ integration/ # Con infraestructura real в”‚ в””в”Ђв”Ђ e2e/ # Flujos completos в”‚ в”њв”Ђв”Ђ pyproject.toml в”њв”Ђв”Ђ Dockerfile в””в”Ђв”Ђ README.md

Las entidades son objetos de negocio puros. Los repositorios son interfaces (Protocols), no implementaciones. src/domain/entities/document.py from __future__ import annotations from dataclasses import dataclass, field from datetime import datetime from enum import Enum from uuid import UUID, uuid4 class DocumentStatus(Enum): PENDING = "pending" INDEXED = "indexed" FAILED = "failed" @dataclass class Document: """Entidad de dominio вЂ” sin dependencias externas.""" id: UUID = field(default_factory=uuid4) title: str = "" content: str = "" source_url: str | None = None status: DocumentStatus = DocumentStatus.PENDING created_at: datetime = field(default_factory=datetime.utcnow) metadata: dict = field(default_factory=dict) def mark_indexed(self) -> None: if self.status == DocumentStatus.FAILED: raise ValueError("Cannot index a failed document without reset") self.status = DocumentStatus.INDEXED def is_ready_for_indexing(self) -> bool: return bool(self.content.strip()) and self.status == DocumentStatus.PENDING src/domain/repositories/vector_store.py вЂ” Interfaces (Protocol) from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable @runtime_checkable class VectorStore(Protocol): """ Interfaz del vector store. El dominio define QUГ‰ necesita, la infra define CГ“MO. """ async def index_chunks(self, chunks: list[Chunk]) -> None: ... async def search(self, query_embedding: list[float], top_k: int = 5) -> list[SearchResult]: ... async def delete_by_document_id(self, document_id: str) -> None: ... @runtime_checkable class LLMGateway(Protocol): async def complete(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> str: ... async def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]: ...

Los casos de uso contienen la lГіgica de aplicaciГіn. Reciben interfaces a travГ©s de inyecciГіn de dependencias, nunca instancian infraestructura directamente. src/application/use_cases/query_rag.py from dataclasses import dataclass from ...domain.repositories.vector_store import VectorStore, LLMGateway @dataclass class QueryRAGRequest: question: str conversation_history: list[dict] = None top_k: int = 5 temperature: float = 0.3 @dataclass class QueryRAGResponse: answer: str sources: list[str] confidence: float class QueryRAGUseCase: """ Solo conoce interfaces, no implementaciones concretas. Se puede cambiar OpenAI por Anthropic sin tocar esta clase. """ def __init__( self, vector_store: VectorStore, # Interface, no Pinecone concreto llm: LLMGateway, # Interface, no OpenAI concreto ): self._vector_store = vector_store self._llm = llm async def execute(self, request: QueryRAGRequest) -> QueryRAGResponse: # 1. Embed la query [query_embedding] = await self._llm.embed([request.question]) # 2. Recuperar chunks relevantes results = await self._vector_store.search( query_embedding, top_k=request.top_k ) if not results: return QueryRAGResponse( answer="No encontrГ© informaciГіn relevante.", sources=[], confidence=0.0 ) # 3. Construir contexto y prompt context = "

---

".join([r.text for r in results]) sources = list({r.source_url for r in results if r.source_url}) messages = [ {"role": "system", "content": f"Responde SOLO basГЎndote en:

{context}"}, *(request.conversation_history or []), {"role": "user", "content": request.question} ] # 4. Generar respuesta answer = await self._llm.complete(messages, temperature=request.temperature) avg_score = sum(r.score for r in results) / len(results) return QueryRAGResponse(answer=answer, sources=sources, confidence=avg_score) Por quГ© esto es poderoso Este caso de uso no sabe si el vector store es Pinecone, Qdrant o una lista en memoria. Puedes cambiar cualquiera sin tocar este archivo.

Los adaptadores traducen entre el "mundo externo" y los casos de uso. No contienen lГіgica de negocio. src/adapters/api/routers/chat.py from fastapi import APIRouter, Depends, HTTPException from pydantic import BaseModel from ...application.use_cases.query_rag import QueryRAGUseCase, QueryRAGRequest from ...infrastructure.config.container import get_query_rag_use_case router = APIRouter(prefix="/chat", tags=["chat"]) class ChatRequest(BaseModel): question: str conversation_history: list[dict] = [] class ChatResponse(BaseModel): answer: str sources: list[str] confidence: float @router.post("/", response_model=ChatResponse) async def chat( request: ChatRequest, use_case: QueryRAGUseCase = Depends(get_query_rag_use_case) ): # Adapter: HTTP request в†’ use case request result = await use_case.execute(QueryRAGRequest( question=request.question, conversation_history=request.conversation_history, )) # Adapter: use case response в†’ HTTP response return ChatResponse(answer=result.answer, sources=result.sources, confidence=result.confidence)

src/infrastructure/llm/openai_gateway.py from openai import AsyncOpenAI class OpenAIGateway: """Implementa LLMGateway con OpenAI. El dominio NUNCA importa esto.""" def __init__(self, api_key: str, model: str = "gpt-4o"): self._client = AsyncOpenAI(api_key=api_key) self._model = model async def complete(self, messages: list[dict], **kwargs) -> str: response = await self._client.chat.completions.create( model=self._model, messages=messages, **kwargs ) return response.choices[0].message.content async def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]: response = await self._client.embeddings.create( model="text-embedding-3-small", input=texts ) return [e.embedding for e in response.data] src/infrastructure/config/container.py вЂ” El 'pegamento' from functools import lru_cache from .settings import Settings from ..llm.openai_gateway import OpenAIGateway from ..vector_stores.qdrant_store import QdrantVectorStore from ...application.use_cases.query_rag import QueryRAGUseCase @lru_cache def get_settings() -> Settings: return Settings() def get_query_rag_use_case() -> QueryRAGUseCase: # Para cambiar de Qdrant a Pinecone: solo cambias esta lГ­nea return QueryRAGUseCase( vector_store=QdrantVectorStore(url=get_settings().qdrant_url), llm=OpenAIGateway(api_key=get_settings().openai_api_key) )
documents.s: в‘Ј Infraestructura e InyecciГіn de Dependencias

documents.t: La mayor ventaja: los casos de uso se testean con mocks, sin infraestructura real. tests/unit/application/test_query_rag.py import pytest from unittest.mock import AsyncMock from src.application.use_cases.query_rag import QueryRAGUseCase, QueryRAGRequest @pytest.mark.asyncio async def test_returns_answer_with_sources(): # Arrange: mocks puros, sin API keys ni conexiones mock_vs = AsyncMock() mock_vs.search.return_value = [ SearchResult(text="Python es interpretado.", score=0.95, source_url="https://python.org"), ] mock_llm = AsyncMock() mock_llm.embed.return_value = [[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]] mock_llm.complete.return_value = "Python es un lenguaje interpretado." use_case = QueryRAGUseCase(vector_store=mock_vs, llm=mock_llm) # Act response = await use_case.execute(QueryRAGRequest(question="ВїQuГ© es Python?")) # Assert assert "Python" in response.answer assert len(response.sources) == 1 assert response.confidence > 0.8 mock_llm.complete.assert_called_once() @pytest.mark.asyncio async def test_handles_empty_results(): mock_vs = AsyncMock() mock_vs.search.return_value = [] # Sin resultados mock_llm = AsyncMock() mock_llm.embed.return_value = [[0.1, 0.2]] use_case = QueryRAGUseCase(vector_store=mock_vs, llm=mock_llm) response = await use_case.execute(QueryRAGRequest(question="Pregunta sin respuesta")) assert response.confidence == 0.0 mock_llm.complete.assert_not_called() # No llamar al LLM sin contexto

SituaciГіn вќЊ Sin Clean Arch вњ… Con Clean Arch Cambiar de OpenAI a Anthropic Modificar 15+ archivos Crear AnthropicGateway, cambiar 1 lГ­nea en container.py Testear lГіgica RAG Necesitas API keys reales Mocks puros, tests en < 1 segundo Cambiar de Pinecone a Qdrant Refactoring masivo Crear QdrantStore, cambiar container.py AГ±adir CLI ademГЎs de HTTP Duplicar lГіgica Nuevo adapter que usa el mismo use case Onboarding de nuevo dev "El cГіdigo estГЎ en varios sitios" Estructura predecible y documentada CuГЎndo NO usar Clean Architecture Para prototipos rГЎpidos, scripts one-off o proyectos de menos de 2 semanas. Clean Architecture aГ±ade overhead innecesario en estos casos.

Recurso Tipo Clean Architecture вЂ” Robert C. Martin Libro fundacional The Clean Architecture (blog) Blog post original Architecture Patterns with Python Libro gratuito online Hexagonal Architecture Paper original FastAPI Clean Architecture Example GitHub

AI Engineer GuГ­a tГ©cnica completa para convertirte en un AI Engineer de nivel profesional. Cubre desde los fundamentos de Python y matemГЎticas hasta el despliegue de sistemas LLM en producciГіn.

Un AI Engineer implementa, optimiza y despliega modelos de inteligencia artificial. Se diferencia del ML Scientist en que su foco estГЎ en llevar los modelos a producciГіn de forma robusta, escalable y mantenible.

Fase DuraciГіn Temas 1. Fundamentos 3вЂ“6 meses Python, MatemГЎticas, Data Eng, Software Eng 2. ML Core 3вЂ“6 meses ML ClГЎsico, Deep Learning, EvaluaciГіn, Features 3. LLMs & GenAI 3вЂ“5 meses Transformers, RAG, Prompting, Agents, Fine-tuning 4. MLOps 2вЂ“4 meses Serving, Pipelines, Monitoring, Cloud 5. EspecializaciГіn Continuo AI Safety, LLM Evaluation, Dominio vertical
Si vienes de cero en programaciГіn в†’ empieza por Python Avanzado. Si ya sabes programar в†’ ve directo a ML ClГЎsico. Si ya conoces ML в†’ salta a LLMs y Transformers.

AI Engineer В· Fase 1 Dominar Python a nivel avanzado es el punto de partida obligatorio para cualquier AI Engineer. No basta con saber el lenguaje: necesitas entender sus internals para escribir cГіdigo eficiente, mantenible y apto para producciГіn. Tags: Python 3.10+ В· NumPy В· Pandas В· asyncio В· typing

A partir de Python 3.10, el sistema de tipos es maduro y potente. Usar tipado estГЎtico mejora la mantenibilidad, detecta bugs temprano y facilita el trabajo en equipo. Tipos avanzados en Python 3.11 from __future__ import annotations from typing import TypeVar, Generic, Protocol from dataclasses import dataclass, field import numpy as np from numpy.typing import NDArray # TypeVar para generics en clases T = TypeVar("T") FloatArray = NDArray[np.float64] @dataclass class ModelOutput: embeddings: FloatArray logits: FloatArray metadata: dict[str, float] = field(default_factory=dict) # Protocol para duck typing estructural class Vectorizer(Protocol): def encode(self, texts: list[str]) -> FloatArray: ... def encode_batch(self, texts: list[str], batch_size: int = 32) -> FloatArray: ... def process_embeddings( model: Vectorizer, texts: list[str], *, # keyword-only arguments normalize: bool = True, dtype: np.dtype = np.float32 ) -> FloatArray: embeddings = model.encode(texts) if normalize: norms = np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=-1, keepdims=True) embeddings = embeddings / (norms + 1e-8) return embeddings.astype(dtype)

Cuando construyes pipelines que llaman a APIs de LLMs, el cГіdigo asГ­ncrono puede multiplicar el throughput por 10xвЂ“50x al procesar mГєltiples requests en paralelo. Llamadas paralelas a LLM API import asyncio import httpx from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential @retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(3), wait=wait_exponential(min=1, max=10)) async def call_llm(client: httpx.AsyncClient, prompt: str) -> str: response = await client.post( "https://api.openai.com/v1/chat/completions", json={"model": "gpt-4o", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]}, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {API_KEY}"} ) response.raise_for_status() return response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"] async def batch_process(prompts: list[str], concurrency: int = 10) -> list[str]: semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(concurrency) # LГ­mite de concurrencia async def bounded_call(client, prompt): async with semaphore: return await call_llm(client, prompt) async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client: tasks = [bounded_call(client, p) for p in prompts] return await asyncio.gather(*tasks) # 100 prompts en ~2s en lugar de ~200s secuencialmente results = asyncio.run(batch_process(prompts, concurrency=20)) Regla de oro del async Гљsalo cuando tienes IO-bound operations (llamadas a APIs, lecturas de disco, queries a DB). No para CPU-bound como entrenamiento de modelos вЂ” para eso usa multiprocessing.

Entender NumPy es crucial para evitar loops lentos. El principio: nunca iterar elemento a elemento cuando puedes usar broadcasting. import numpy as np # вќЊ LENTO: loop Python вЂ” O(N) llamadas Python similarities = [] for emb in embeddings: sim = np.dot(query, emb) / (np.linalg.norm(query) * np.linalg.norm(emb)) similarities.append(sim) # вњ… RГЃPIDO: operaciГіn vectorizada вЂ” ~100x mГЎs rГЎpido # query: (768,) | embeddings: (N, 768) query_norm = query / np.linalg.norm(query) emb_norms = embeddings / np.linalg.norm(embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True) similarities = query_norm @ emb_norms.T # (N,)

Regla de oro Nunca optimices sin medir. Primero haz funcionar el cГіdigo, luego identifica los cuellos de botella reales con profiling. # Profiling con cProfile python -m cProfile -s cumulative train.py # Line profiler (pip install line-profiler) kernprof -l -v script.py

Software Architecture Esta ruta estГЎ diseГ±ada para pasar de implementar features a diseГ±ar sistemas: claros en su dominio, resilientes en producciГіn y sostenibles para equipos grandes.

Un/a Software Architect: define lГ­mites entre componentes y equipos; toma decisiones de trade-off (latencia, costo, complejidad, time-to-market); establece estГЎndares de diseГ±o, calidad y operaciГіn; reduce riesgo tГ©cnico en decisiones de largo plazo.

Fase DuraciГіn sugerida Objetivo Phase 1 вЂ” Foundations 4вЂ“8 semanas Entender atributos de calidad y patrones base Phase 2 вЂ” Modeling & Boundaries 6вЂ“10 semanas Modelar dominio y contratos entre servicios Phase 3 вЂ” Data & Scale 6вЂ“10 semanas DiseГ±ar consistencia, escalabilidad y confiabilidad Phase 4 вЂ” Security & Operations Continuo Asegurar entrega segura, gobernanza y evoluciГіn

Empieza por system-design-principles y aterriza cada concepto en tu sistema actual. En cada mГіdulo, implementa al menos 1 ADR (Architecture Decision Record). Cierra cada fase con un mini-proyecto de rediseГ±o sobre un caso real.

Al finalizar, podrГЎs: diseГ±ar un sistema end-to-end (desde dominio hasta operaciГіn); justificar decisiones de arquitectura con mГ©tricas y riesgo; guiar equipos para mantener una arquitectura evolutiva.
Modeling & Boundaries Una buena arquitectura se rompe primero en los contratos. DiseГ±ar APIs robustas reduce fricciГіn entre equipos.

Versiona contratos (/v1, headers o schema registry). DiseГ±a para evoluciГіn: campos opcionales y compatibilidad backward. Define idempotencia en operaciones crГ­ticas (Idempotency-Key). Observa contratos en runtime (errores, latencia, saturaciГіn).
Sync (HTTP/gRPC) para respuestas inmediatas. Async (event bus) para procesos largos y desacoplados. Saga/Orchestration para flujos distribuidos con compensaciones.
"API Design Patterns" вЂ” JJ Geewax RFC 7231 (HTTP Semantics)
Foundations Elegir patrГіn no es una preferencia estГ©tica: define acoplamiento, velocidad de entrega y costos operativos.

PatrГіn Гљtil cuando Riesgo principal Monolito modular Equipo pequeГ±o/mediano, dominio estable Convertirse en "big ball of mud" Microservicios Dominios bien separados, equipos autГіnomos Complejidad operativa y de observabilidad Event-driven Procesos desacoplados y asincronГ­a Consistencia eventual mal gestionada Hexagonal Necesidad de aislar dominio del framework SobreingenierГ­a temprana

Contexto: SaaS B2B, 8 engineers, una sola base de datos, releases semanales DecisiГіn: Monolito modular + eventos internos RazГіn: Maximiza velocidad sin pagar costo operativo de microservicios EvoluciГіn: Extraer servicios solo donde exista cuello de botella organizacional o tГ©cnico
Un deploy rompe ГЎreas no relacionadas. El tiempo de build/release crece de forma sostenida. Un equipo bloquea a otros equipos por dependencias.
"Fundamentals of Software Architecture" вЂ” Richards/Ford "Clean Architecture" вЂ” Robert C. Martin
Data & Scale La escalabilidad real depende de cГіmo modelas datos y consistencia, no solo del nГєmero de rГ©plicas.

SQL vs NoSQL segГєn patrГіn de acceso, no por moda. Strong vs Eventual consistency segГєn impacto de negocio. CQRS cuando lectura y escritura tienen necesidades muy distintas. Outbox Pattern para publicar eventos sin perder consistencia.
TransacciГіn Гєnica: 1) INSERT order 2) INSERT outbox_event(order_created) Worker asГ­ncrono: 3) Lee outbox 4) Publica evento a broker 5) Marca evento como enviado
Reintentos sin idempotencia. Eventos sin versionado de schema. Consultas cross-context acopladas a tablas ajenas.
"Designing Data-Intensive Applications" вЂ” Martin Kleppmann "Building Microservices" вЂ” Sam Newman
Este mГіdulo organiza estructuras de datos en dos bloques principales: 1-linear 2-non-linear
Linear structures: secuenciales, recorrido directo. Non-linear structures: jerГЎrquicas o en red, mГєltiples caminos.

An array stores contiguous elements in memory, allowing access by index in constant time. It's basically the вЂњHello WorldвЂќ of data structures: simple, straightforward, and the basis for almost everything we use in programming.

Homogeneity: All elements must be of the same type (all integers, all characters, etc.). Random Access: You can jump directly to any element if you know its index. You don't have to go through it from the beginning. Fixed Size: Traditionally, once you define the size of an array in memory, you cannot change it (although modern languages use вЂњdynamic arraysвЂќ to simulate this).

рџ‘Ќ Advantages: Access speed: It is extremely fast for reading data. Simple iteration: It is very easy to traverse with a simple for loop. Memory efficiency: It does not waste space on extra вЂњpointersвЂќ like linked lists. рџ‘Ћ Disadvantages: Inflexibility: If you run out of space, you have to create a new, larger array and copy everything. Editing cost: Inserting or deleting elements in the middle is computationally expensive.

example-array.py import array int_array = array.array('i', [1, 2, 3, 4]) float_array = array.array('f', [1.1, 2.2, 3.3]) print(int_array) # array('i', [1, 2, 3, 4]) print(float_array) # array('f', [1.1, 2.2, 3.3]) Access by index: int_array[0] Result 1 int_array.append(5) # add at the end: [1,2,3,4,5] int_array.insert(1, 10) # Insert 10 in index 1: [1,10,2,3,4,5] int_array.pop(2) # remove index 2: [1,10,3,4,5] print(int_array[1:4]) # slice: array('i', [10,3,4])
An ArrayList (also known as a Dynamic Array) is a data structure that uses an internal Array to store elements, but has the ability to automatically resize itself.

Use an Array if: The size of your data is constant and known (e.g., the months of the year). Extreme performance and memory savings are critical (embedded systems). Use an ArrayList if: You don't know how many elements you will receive (e.g., a list of users from a database). You need convenient built-in methods to easily search, sort, or delete.
documents.s: When to use an array and when to use an arraylist?

documents.t: Python does not have ArrayList like Java, but lists act as dynamic lists of variable size, equivalent to ArrayList. They are mutable, ordered, and support fast append/remove operations. # empty list my_list = [] # Initialize with values my_list = [1, 2, 3, "text1", 4.5] print(my_list) # [1, 2, 3, 'text1', 4.5] Access by index: my_lista[0] Result 1. Some common operations: Add: my_list.append(99) в†’ adds to the end. Insert: my_list.insert(2, 50) в†’ at position 2. Delete: my_list.pop(1) в†’ removes index 1; my_list.remove(3) в†’ first 3. Size: len(my_list).

A Deque, short for Double-Ended Queue, is a linear data structure that allows you to insert and delete elements from both the front and the rear. Think of it as a hybrid between a Stack and a Queue. Depending on how you use it, it can behave like either one: If you only use one end, itвЂ™s a Stack. If you insert at one end and remove from the other, itвЂ™s a Queue.

A Deque provides four primary operations, all of which typically run in O(1) time: Insert Front: Adds an item to the beginning of the Deque. Insert Rear: Adds an item to the end (like a normal Queue). Delete Front: Removes the first item. Delete Rear: Removes the last item (like a Stack). Additional helper methods: getFront / getRear: Inspects the elements at the ends without removing them. isEmpty / isFull: Checks the status of the underlying storage.

Why would you need a Deque instead of a simple Queue? Undo/Redo History: Many software applications use Deques to manage history. You add new actions to the end, but if the history gets too long, the oldest actions are removed from the front to save memory. A-Steal Scheduling: In multi-core processing, if one processor finishes its tasks, it can "steal" a task from the back of another processor's Deque to keep the system balanced. Palindrome Checker: Because you can remove characters from both ends simultaneously, Deques are the go-to structure for checking if a word is the same backward and forward. Web Browser Cache: Recent URLs are added to the front; if the cache limit is reached, the least recently used (LRU) URL is removed from the rear.

If the Array was a fixed row of boxes and the ArrayList was an elastic row, the LinkedList is something completely different: imagine a treasure hunt. Each clue gives you an object and a piece of paper that tells you where to find the next clue.

A Linear LinkedList is a collection of elements called Nodes. Unlike arrays, nodes are not together in memory; they can be scattered anywhere. What keeps them together is that each node knows who its successor is. Anatomy of a Node A node is divided into two main parts: Data: The value you want to store (number, text, object). Next (Pointer/Reference): The memory address of the next node in the list. The last node in the list points to null, which indicates the end of the chain.

Python doesn't have built-in linked lists like some languages, but you can implement a singly linked list using a Node class with a data value and next pointer. This structure allows efficient insertions/deletions without fixed sizing, unlike lists or arrays. class Node: def __init__(self, data): self.data = data self.next = None class LinkedList: def __init__(self): self.head = None def append(self, data): new_node = Node(data) if not self.head: self.head = new_node return current = self.head while current.next: current = current.next current.next = new_node def print_list(self): current = self.head while current: print(current.data, end=" -> ") current = current.next print("None") Usage Example llist = LinkedList() llist.append(1) llist.append(2) llist.append(3) llist.print_list() # Output: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> None The head points to the first node; each next links to the subsequent one until None. Insertion at Beginning def insert_at_beginning(self, data): new_node = Node(data) new_node.next = self.head self.head = new_node Add this method to LinkedList for O(1) prepends.

Although we are talking about the linear form, there are three common variants: Singly Linked List (Simple): Each node points only to the next one. (One direction). Doubly Linked List (Double): Each node points to the next and the previous one. (Two directions). Circular Linked List: The last node points back to the first one, forming a ring.

A queue is a linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First-In, First-Out) principle, which means that the first item to enter is the first to leave. It is the fairest structure that exists: the item that has been waiting the longest is the one that is processed first.

Unlike a Stack (where everything happens at the вЂњTopвЂќ), in a Queue we work with two ends: the Front and the Rear/Back. Enqueue: Adds an element to the end of the queue. Dequeue: Removes and returns the element at the front of the queue. Front (or Peek): Looks at who is first in line without removing them. isEmpty: Checks if there is no one waiting.

Sometimes a simple queue is not enough, so there are variants: Priority Queue: The first item to arrive is not the first to leave, but rather the one that is вЂњmost importantвЂќ (like a hospital emergency room). Deque (Double-Ended Queue): You can insert and remove items from both the front and the end. It is a hybrid between a Stack and a Queue. Circular Queue: The last position connects to the first to take advantage of the memory gaps left when someone leaves the queue.

Queues are vital for managing shared resources: Printers: Documents are printed in the order they were sent. Web servers: When thousands of people visit a site, the server puts them in a queue to handle requests one by one. Operating Systems: To decide which process should use the processor (CPU Scheduling). Video Buffers: When you watch YouTube, the video is downloaded into a queue so that you always have fragments ready to play.

A stack is a linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) principle, which means that the last element to enter is the first to leave. You cannot remove an element from the middle without first removing the ones above it. It is a restricted structure, but that restriction is precisely what makes it so powerful for certain problems.

Unlike arrays, where you can access any index, in a stack we only interact with the top. Push: Adds an element to the top of the stack. Pop: Removes and returns the element at the top. Peek (or Top): View the top element without removing it. isEmpty: Check if the stack is empty.

Stacks are everywhere, even if you can't see them: The вЂњBackвЂќ button on your browser: Each page you visit is pushed onto the stack. When you go back, it pops off the stack. Undo (Ctrl + Z): Text editors save your changes on a stack. When you undo, they remove the last change. Recursion: When a function calls itself, the computer uses a вЂњCall StackвЂќ to remember where each call left off. Balancing parentheses: Compilers use stacks to check that each ( has its ).

Python does not have native stacks, but you can implement one using lists with append() and pop() for LIFO (Last In, First Out). Here is a complete example of a Stack class. class Stack: def __init__(self): self.items = [] # intern list def push(self, item): self.items.append(item) # Add to "top" def pop(self): if self.is_empty(): raise Exception("Stack vacГ­o") return self.items.pop() # Remove "top" def peek(self): if self.is_empty(): raise Exception("Stack vacГ­o") return self.items[-1] # Look at the top without removing it def is_empty(self): return len(self.items) == 0 def size(self): return len(self.items)

stack = Stack() # Push elements stack.push(1) stack.push(2) stack.push(3) print(stack.size()) # 3 # Peek (look top) print(stack.peek()) # 3 # Pop (remove top) print(stack.pop()) # 3 print(stack.pop()) # 2 print(stack.is_empty()) # False
def __str__(self): return str(self.items[::-1]) # Invest to show top first print(stack) # [1] after pops
Un BST es un binary tree con regla de orden: nodos a la izquierda < nodo actual nodos a la derecha > nodo actual
BГєsqueda/inserciГіn/eliminaciГіn promedio: O(log n). Peor caso (desbalanceado): O(n).
Un binary tree es una estructura jerГЎrquica donde cada nodo tiene como mГЎximo dos hijos: izquierdo y derecho.
Recorridos comunes: preorden, inorden, postorden. BГєsqueda en ГЎrbol general: O(n). Base para heaps, BST y ГЎrboles balanceados.
Security & Operations Una arquitectura madura evoluciona con reglas claras: decisiones explГ­citas, estГЎndares y feedback operacional.

ADRs para decisiones relevantes. Tech Radar trimestral (adopt/trial/assess/hold). Definition of Done arquitectГіnico (tests, observabilidad, seguridad). Revisiones de arquitectura ligeras y frecuentes.

# ADR-012: Adoptar API Gateway Fecha: 2026-02-23 Contexto: crecimiento de integraciones externas y seguridad inconsistente DecisiГіn: centralizar auth, rate limiting y observabilidad en gateway Consecuencias: - (+) polГ­ticas consistentes y menor duplicaciГіn - (-) nuevo punto crГ­tico operativo
Lead time de cambios Change failure rate MTTR deuda tГ©cnica priorizada/cerrada por trimestre
Modeling & Boundaries DDD ayuda a convertir complejidad del negocio en software mantenible, separando contextos y lenguaje.

Bounded Context: frontera donde un modelo tiene significado consistente. Ubiquitous Language: tГ©rminos compartidos entre negocio y tecnologГ­a. Aggregates: unidad de consistencia transaccional. Context Mapping: relaciГіn entre modelos de distintos equipos.

Contextos: - Catalog: productos, categorГ­as, precios pГєblicos - Checkout: carrito, promociones, total - Billing: facturas, impuestos, cobro Regla: Checkout no escribe tablas de Billing directamente. IntegraciГіn: evento "OrderConfirmed" + contrato de API.
Un "Shared Kernel" gigante para todo el negocio. Entidades anГ©micas sin reglas de dominio. Microservicios por capa tГ©cnica, no por dominio.
"Domain-Driven Design" вЂ” Eric Evans "Implementing Domain-Driven Design" вЂ” Vaughn Vernon

Paradigms and fundamental principles Topics to master: First-order functions Pure functions and side effects Immutability Function composition Higher-order functions (map, filter, reduce) Closures Currying and partial application Monads and Functors (conceptually) Recommended resources: Book: вЂњFunctional Programming in ScalaвЂќ by Paul Chiusano Course: вЂњFunctional Programming FundamentalsвЂќ on edX Tutorial: вЂњLearn You a Haskell for Great GoodвЂќ JavaScript ES6+ documentation (accessible for beginners) Practical examples: Use map, filter, reduce in JavaScript/Python to process data Create pure functions that transform data without modifying the original Implement composition: compose(formatUser, validateUser, parseUser) Using closures to create counters or private state Real-world use case: Data processing system: using pure functions to transform user logs Processing pipeline: read data -> validate -> transform -> save (using composition) Report generator using reduce to aggregate data Exercises: Convert imperative operations to functional ones (loops to map/filter/reduce) Create a library of reusable functions (compose, pipe) Implement a small functional library with higher-order functions
Paradigms and fundamental principles In real systems, a single paradigm is rarely used. The hybrid approach combines OOP, functional, and reactive depending on the problem.

OOP for domain modeling and complex rules. Functional for transformations and pure logic. Reactive for orchestrating events and asynchronous workflows. Topics to master: When to mix paradigms OOP + Functional (as in modern Java) Reactive + Functional Domain-driven design oriented

Define clear boundaries between styles. Do not mix paradigms in the same component without reason. Prioritize readability and maintainability of the team. Resources: Articles in specialized blogs Documentation of modern frameworks (Spring Reactive, etc.)

Paradigms and fundamental principles Topics to master: Classes and objects Encapsulation (access modifiers) Inheritance and polymorphism Abstraction Composition vs. inheritance Abstract methods and interfaces Exceptions and error handling Recommended resources: Book: вЂњEffective JavaвЂќ by Joshua Bloch (3rd edition) Course: вЂњObject-Oriented Programming in JavaвЂќ on Coursera Official Java/Python documentation Tutorial: Head First Design Patterns Practical examples: Create a class hierarchy for a banking system (Account, SavingsAccount, CheckingAccount) Implement interfaces for different types of payments (Card, PayPal, Transfer) Design a permission system with proper encapsulation Real-world use case: Employee management system: different types of employees (Executive, Contractor, Intern) with different benefits and responsibilities Implement polymorphism to calculate salaries according to employee type Exercises: Implement a library system with OOP (Book, User, Loan) Create a game with inheritance (Character > Warrior, Mage, Archer) Design an e-commerce site with composition (Cart, Product, Discount)

Paradigms and fundamental principles Topics to master: Observables and Observers Data streams Transformation operators (map, filter, flatMap, merge, etc.) Hot vs Cold observables Error handling in streams Backpressure Marble diagrams Subject (BehaviorSubject, ReplaySubject) Recommended resources: Official RxJS / Project Reactor documentation Book: вЂњLearning RxJavaвЂќ by Nick Pollack Course: вЂњRxJS in PracticeвЂќ on Udemy Interactive tutorial: RxJSMarbles.com Practical examples: Create a reactive autocomplete that searches as the user types Implement a mouse event listener with debounce and throttle Handle multiple streams (user + API data) with merge/combineLatest Create a reactive shopping cart Real-world use case: Real-time dashboard: multiple data sources that are constantly updated Notification system: events that are processed and transformed reactively WebSocket listener: automatically connect/reconnect with retries Exercises: Implement a counter that increments every second and can be reset Create a search system where debounces + cancel if there is another search Simulate a shopping cart with reactive changes to the total
Foundations El diseГ±o arquitectГіnico empieza con atributos de calidad: disponibilidad, latencia, escalabilidad, seguridad, mantenibilidad y costo.

Usa este orden para tomar decisiones: Define SLOs: p95 latencia, disponibilidad, error budget. Identifica cuellos de botella: CPU, IO, red, base de datos. PropГіn opciones y trade-offs. Documenta en ADR con impacto esperado.

Problema: Checkout lento en hora pico OpciГіn A: Escalar vertical DB OpciГіn B: Cache + cola asГ­ncrona + read replicas DecisiГіn: B (mejor costo/escala; mГЎs complejidad operacional) MГ©trica objetivo: p95 < 400ms

Martin "Release It!" вЂ” Michael T. Nygard "Accelerate" вЂ” Nicole Forsgren, Jez Humble, Gene Kim
C4 Model AWS Well-Architected Framework Google Cloud Architecture Framework Azure Well-Architected Framework OWASP ASVS

DiseГ±a un sistema de pagos con SLOs explГ­citos y ADRs. RediseГ±a un monolito a monolito modular con bounded contexts. Implementa un flujo transaccional con Outbox + retries idempotentes. Define un dashboard SRE con p95, error rate, saturation y burn rate.
Data & Scale Escalar no es solo "mГЎs pods": implica capacidad, degradaciГіn controlada y recuperaciГіn rГЎpida.
Timeouts y retries con jitter. Circuit breaker para cortar cascadas de fallos. Bulkheads para aislar recursos. Rate limiting para proteger servicios crГ­ticos. Caching por capa (edge, app, DB).
service: checkout-api timeout_ms: 800 retry: max_attempts: 2 backoff: exponential_jitter circuit_breaker: error_threshold_percent: 50 open_state_seconds: 30
p50/p95/p99 latency error rate por endpoint saturation (CPU/memoria/conexiones) SLO compliance y burn rate
Google SRE Book "Release It!" вЂ” Michael Nygard
Security & Operations La seguridad Гєtil se diseГ±a desde arquitectura: identidad, secretos, cifrado, segmentaciГіn y trazabilidad.
Zero Trust para servicios internos (mTLS + policies). Secret management centralizado (rotaciГіn y auditorГ­a). Principio de menor privilegio en IAM. Threat modeling por dominio crГ­tico.

Activo: datos de pago Amenaza: exfiltraciГіn vГ­a endpoint vulnerable Mitigaciones: - WAF + schema validation - tokenizaciГіn de datos sensibles - cifrado en trГЎnsito y reposo - alertas por patrones anГіmalos
Dependencias con CVEs crГ­ticos. RotaciГіn de llaves y certificados. Logs firmados y trazabilidad de acciones privilegiadas.
